title: Validating Input Formats
concept: Checking tool arguments against schemas before running tool calls. 'Next up: Agent Project: Debugging Loop.'.

exercises.prompt: What is the purpose of the execution wrapper when using the terminal tool?

options.text: To handle input validation before execution
To run commands, cap output, and catch exceptions
To display terminal commands as text

exercises.explain: The execution wrapper is used to run commands safely, manage output, and handle any errors.

cards.title: What is Input Format Validation?
cards.body: Input format validation checks if the inputs for a tool follow specific rules or formats before processing them.

cards.title: Input Schema
cards.body: An input schema is a set of rules that define what inputs are correct, including types and structures expected by a tool.

cards.title: Input Validation as a Key
cards.body: Think of input validation like using a key to unlock a door; only the right key can open the door and let you inside.

cards.title: Example of Input Validation
cards.body: If a tool requires a number, validating input will check if the provided value is truly a number, like ensuring a lock fits before trying to open the door.

cards.title: Common Misconception
cards.body: Some think input validation slows things down, but it actually prevents bigger errors later on, saving time.

cards.title: Valid vs Invalid Inputs
cards.body: Valid inputs follow the schema, while invalid inputs break the rules.

exercises.prompt: Why is input validation important when calling tools?

options.text: It saves time by skipping checks
It prevents bad data from causing errors
It makes the tool run faster
It allows any input to be processed

exercises.explain: Validating inputs ensures that only appropriate data is processed, preventing errors that could occur with bad data.

exercises.prompt: Input validation is only necessary for complex tools. True or False?
exercises.explain: Input validation is important for all tools, regardless of complexity, to ensure reliability.

exercises.prompt: Before running tool calls, we check tool arguments against __________.
exercises.sentence: Before running tool calls, we check tool arguments against schemas.
exercises.explain: Schemas are the rules we check against to ensure inputs are correct before processing.

exercises.prompt: Which of the following is an example of input validation?

options.text: Checking if a user's name is too long
Verifying if a number is positive before processing
Counting the number of inputs for a tool

exercises.explain: Verifying if a number is positive is a direct example of checking input to ensure it meets the requirements.

exercises.prompt: What happens if we skip validating input formats before running tool calls?

options.text: It may result in errors or unexpected results
The call will always succeed without issues
It makes the tool easier to use
Validation is only a suggestion, not required

exercises.explain: Skipping input validation can lead to errors or unexpected behavior because the tool may not work correctly with invalid inputs. Always check inputs against schemas first. Next up: Agent Project: Debugging Loop.
